title: Diabetic Ketoacidosis
clinicalContext: A 28-year-old woman with type 1 diabetes presents to the ED with nausea, vomiting, and abdominal pain for 2 days. She ran out of insulin 3 days ago. She is lethargic, with Kussmaul respirations, fruity breath, dry mucous membranes, and poor skin turgor. VS: BP 95/60, HR 118, RR 28, T 37.8°C. Labs show glucose 485 mg/dL, pH 7.12, HCO3 8 mEq/L, K 5.8 mEq/L, Na 131 mEq/L, anion gap 28, and positive serum ketones.
learningObjectives: Define DKA using the 2024 ADA/EASD consensus criteria
Understand the pathophysiology of insulin deficiency leading to ketoacidosis
Apply the systematic approach to DKA management: fluids, insulin, potassium
Calculate corrected sodium and anion gap
Recognize and manage complications including cerebral edema and hypokalemia
Identify criteria for DKA resolution and safe transition to subcutaneous insulin

guidelineReferences.name: ADA/EASD Consensus Report on Hyperglycemic Crises 2024
guidelineReferences.citation: Umpierrez GE, et al. Hyperglycemic Crises in Adults With Diabetes: A Consensus Report. Diabetes Care. 2024;47(8):1257-1275

guidelineReferences.name: StatPearls: Adult Diabetic Ketoacidosis
guidelineReferences.citation: Ghimire P, Dhamoon AS. Adult Diabetic Ketoacidosis. StatPearls [Internet]. Treasure Island (FL): StatPearls Publishing; 2025

guidelineReferences.name: Endotext: Hyperglycemic Crises
guidelineReferences.citation: Eledrisi M, Elzouki A. Hyperglycemic Crises. Endotext [Internet]. MDText.com; 2025

questions.question: What are the diagnostic criteria for DKA according to the 2024 consensus report?
questions.answer: DKA is defined by the triad: (1) Hyperglycemia: Blood glucose >250 mg/dL (though euglycemic DKA can occur), (2) Metabolic acidosis: pH <7.3 and/or HCO3 <18 mEq/L, (3) Ketosis: Serum beta-hydroxybutyrate ≥3.0 mmol/L (or positive serum/urine ketones). The 2024 update emphasizes beta-hydroxybutyrate measurement as the gold standard over urine ketones.

sources.text: StatPearls: Diabetic Ketoacidosis. NCBI Bookshelf. Accessed January 14, 2026.

whyChain.why: Why is beta-hydroxybutyrate preferred over urine ketones?
whyChain.answer: Beta-hydroxybutyrate is the predominant ketone in DKA (2-3x higher than acetoacetate). Urine dipsticks only detect acetoacetate/acetone using the nitroprusside reaction and MISS beta-hydroxybutyrate. During treatment, beta-hydroxybutyrate converts to acetoacetate, making urine ketones appear to INCREASE even as DKA resolves.

questions.question: How is DKA severity classified?
questions.answer: DKA severity is graded as: Mild: pH 7.25-7.30, HCO3 15-18 mEq/L, alert. Moderate: pH 7.0-7.24, HCO3 10-14 mEq/L, alert/drowsy. Severe: pH <7.0, HCO3 <10 mEq/L, stupor/coma. Severe DKA or DKA with altered mental status requires ICU admission.

sources.text: StatPearls: Diabetic Ketoacidosis. NCBI Bookshelf. Accessed January 14, 2026.

whyChain.why: Can mild DKA be managed outside the ICU?
whyChain.answer: Yes. The 2024 consensus supports managing mild and uncomplicated moderate DKA with subcutaneous insulin in non-critical care settings when patients are hemodynamically stable, alert, and able to tolerate oral intake. This approach is safe, cost-effective, and reduces ICU utilization.
whyChain.source: ADA/EASD Consensus Report on Hyperglycemic Crises 2024

questions.question: What is euglycemic DKA, and what causes it?
questions.answer: Euglycemic DKA is ketoacidosis with glucose <250 mg/dL. Causes include: (1) SGLT2 inhibitors (block renal glucose reabsorption, promote glucosuria, reduce insulin secretion, increase glucagon), (2) Pregnancy (increased metabolic demands, placental glucose consumption), (3) Starvation/fasting, (4) Recent insulin administration, (5) Alcohol use. Treatment is the same but requires dextrose from the start.

sources.text: StatPearls: Diabetic Ketoacidosis. NCBI Bookshelf. Accessed January 14, 2026.

whyChain.why: Why do SGLT2 inhibitors cause euglycemic DKA?
whyChain.answer: SGLT2 inhibitors lower glucose (allowing lower insulin doses), but they also increase glucagon secretion from pancreatic alpha cells (SGLT2 is expressed there) and may reduce urinary ketone excretion. The combination of reduced insulin and increased glucagon promotes lipolysis and ketogenesis despite normal glucose.

layers.name: Pathophysiology
layers.description: Understanding how insulin deficiency leads to DKA

questions.question: What is the pathophysiology of ketoacidosis in insulin deficiency?
questions.answer: Insulin deficiency + counter-regulatory hormone excess (glucagon, cortisol, epinephrine, growth hormone) causes: (1) Hyperglycemia from hepatic gluconeogenesis/glycogenolysis and decreased peripheral glucose uptake, (2) Lipolysis releasing free fatty acids from adipose tissue, (3) Hepatic beta-oxidation of FFAs generating acetyl-CoA, (4) Ketogenesis when acetyl-CoA exceeds Krebs cycle capacity, producing acetoacetate, beta-hydroxybutyrate, and acetone.

sources.text: StatPearls: Diabetic Ketoacidosis. NCBI Bookshelf. Accessed January 14, 2026.
Kitabchi AE, et al. Hyperglycemic Crises in Adult Patients With Diabetes. Diabetes Care. 2009;32(7):1335-1343. Accessed January 14, 2026.

whyChain.why: Why does insulin deficiency cause lipolysis?
whyChain.answer: Insulin normally SUPPRESSES hormone-sensitive lipase in adipocytes. Without insulin, this enzyme is activated, hydrolyzing triglycerides into glycerol and free fatty acids. Glucagon further stimulates lipolysis. The released FFAs are then taken up by the liver for beta-oxidation and ketone production.

questions.question: Why does DKA cause volume depletion and electrolyte abnormalities?
questions.answer: Hyperglycemia exceeds the renal threshold (~180 mg/dL), causing osmotic diuresis with massive urinary losses of water, sodium, potassium, magnesium, and phosphate. Typical deficits in DKA: Water 5-7 L, Na 500-700 mEq, K 300-500 mEq (despite often normal/high serum K). Vomiting compounds losses. The hyperosmolar state also draws water from intracellular to extracellular space.

sources.text: StatPearls: Diabetic Ketoacidosis. NCBI Bookshelf. Accessed January 14, 2026.
Kitabchi AE, et al. Hyperglycemic Crises in Adult Patients With Diabetes. Diabetes Care. 2009;32(7):1335-1343. Accessed January 14, 2026.

whyChain.why: Why is serum potassium often normal or high despite total body depletion?
whyChain.answer: Three mechanisms shift K+ from intracellular to extracellular: (1) Insulin deficiency - insulin normally drives K+ into cells, (2) Acidosis - H+ enters cells in exchange for K+, (3) Hyperosmolality - water leaves cells, K+ follows. As these are corrected, K+ shifts back into cells, unmasking the true deficit.

questions.question: How do you calculate and interpret the anion gap in DKA?
questions.answer: Anion gap = Na - (Cl + HCO3). Normal is 8-12 mEq/L. In DKA, ketone bodies (beta-hydroxybutyrate, acetoacetate) are unmeasured anions that increase the gap. An elevated anion gap (typically >14-15) indicates accumulation of these acids. As DKA resolves, the gap normalizes. Note: The delta gap (change in anion gap / change in bicarbonate) should be ~1:1 in pure DKA.

sources.text: StatPearls: Diabetic Ketoacidosis. NCBI Bookshelf. Accessed January 14, 2026.
Kitabchi AE, et al. Hyperglycemic Crises in Adult Patients With Diabetes. Diabetes Care. 2009;32(7):1335-1343. Accessed January 14, 2026.

whyChain.why: What if the anion gap closes but bicarbonate is still low?
whyChain.answer: This indicates a non-anion gap metabolic acidosis (NAGMA), typically hyperchloremic acidosis. In DKA, this develops from: (1) Aggressive normal saline resuscitation (chloride load), (2) Urinary ketone excretion (losing bicarbonate equivalents). NAGMA delays apparent 'resolution' but is usually self-correcting.

layers.name: Initial Assessment
layers.description: Evaluation and workup of the DKA patient

questions.question: What is the essential initial workup for suspected DKA?
questions.answer: Essential labs: Glucose, BMP (electrolytes, BUN, creatinine), VBG or ABG (pH, pCO2), serum ketones (beta-hydroxybutyrate if available), CBC, urinalysis. Calculate anion gap and corrected sodium. Additional workup to identify precipitant: HbA1c, chest X-ray, ECG, lipase, troponin, blood/urine cultures if infection suspected.

sources.text: StatPearls: Diabetic Ketoacidosis. NCBI Bookshelf. Accessed January 14, 2026.

whyChain.why: Is an ABG necessary, or is VBG sufficient?
whyChain.answer: VBG is usually sufficient and is emphasized in the 2024 guidelines. Venous pH correlates well with arterial pH (typically 0.02-0.04 lower). ABG is only needed if respiratory status is in question or precise pCO2 assessment is required. This reduces patient discomfort and resource utilization.
whyChain.source: ADA/EASD Consensus Report on Hyperglycemic Crises 2024

questions.question: How do you calculate and interpret corrected sodium in DKA?
questions.answer: Corrected Na = Measured Na + [1.6 × (Glucose - 100)/100]. This accounts for dilutional hyponatremia from osmotic water shifts. If corrected sodium is LOW: true hyponatremia, continue 0.9% NS. If corrected sodium is NORMAL or HIGH: switch to 0.45% NS after initial resuscitation. Corrected sodium should RISE as glucose falls; failure to rise suggests excess free water.

sources.text: StatPearls: Diabetic Ketoacidosis. NCBI Bookshelf. Accessed January 14, 2026.

whyChain.why: Why is failure of sodium to rise concerning?
whyChain.answer: As glucose falls and water shifts back into cells, serum sodium should rise. If it doesn't, excess free water is being administered. This raises risk of cerebral edema, especially in children. Target a rise in sodium of 0.5 mEq/L for every 1 mmol/L drop in glucose.

questions.question: What are the common precipitants of DKA that should be identified?
questions.answer: The 'I's of DKA precipitants: Infection (most common - UTI, pneumonia, sepsis), Insulin omission/non-compliance (second most common), Infarction (MI, stroke), Inflammation (pancreatitis), Intoxication, Iatrogenic (steroids, SGLT2 inhibitors), Initial presentation of new-onset diabetes (20-25% of cases). Always search for and treat the underlying cause.

sources.text: StatPearls: Diabetic Ketoacidosis. NCBI Bookshelf. Accessed January 14, 2026.

whyChain.why: Can infection cause DKA even with normal WBC and fever?
whyChain.answer: Yes. Infection may be present without fever (especially in severe DKA with hypothermia) and WBC is often elevated from stress/dehydration. Maintain high suspicion for infection. The stress response and counterregulatory hormones from infection directly antagonize insulin action.

layers.name: Fluid Resuscitation
layers.description: Evidence-based approach to IV fluids in DKA

questions.question: What is the recommended approach to fluid resuscitation in DKA?
questions.answer: Initial bolus: 0.9% NS at 15-20 mL/kg/hr (1-1.5 L) in the first hour to restore perfusion. Subsequent fluids: 250-500 mL/hr (4-14 mL/kg/hr) based on hemodynamics and corrected sodium. If corrected Na is normal/high, switch to 0.45% NS. Goal: Replace ~50% of deficit in first 8-12 hours, total deficit (typically 5-7 L) over 24-36 hours.

sources.text: StatPearls: Diabetic Ketoacidosis. NCBI Bookshelf. Accessed January 14, 2026.

whyChain.why: Should balanced crystalloids be used instead of normal saline?
whyChain.answer: Recent evidence suggests balanced solutions (LR, Plasmalyte) may resolve DKA faster (by ~5 hours) with less hyperchloremic acidosis. However, the 2024 guidelines still list 0.9% NS as first-line. Balanced crystalloids can be considered, especially in patients with baseline renal dysfunction or prolonged resuscitation.

questions.question: When should dextrose be added to IV fluids?
questions.answer: Add dextrose (D5W or D10W) when glucose falls to <250 mg/dL. This is critical because: (1) Allows continued insulin infusion to clear ketones (ketosis takes longer to resolve than hyperglycemia), (2) Prevents hypoglycemia, (3) Provides substrate for cellular metabolism. Target glucose 150-200 mg/dL until DKA resolves.

sources.text: StatPearls: Diabetic Ketoacidosis. NCBI Bookshelf. Accessed January 14, 2026.

whyChain.why: Why does ketosis take longer to resolve than hyperglycemia?
whyChain.answer: Glucose drops quickly with insulin (promoting cellular uptake) and fluids (dilution + osmotic diuresis). Ketones require: (1) Insulin to suppress lipolysis and halt new ketone production, (2) Peripheral metabolism of existing ketones. Beta-hydroxybutyrate half-life is several hours even with treatment.
whyChain.source: Diagnosis and Treatment of DKA and HHS. CMAJ. 2003

questions.question: What is the recommended insulin regimen for DKA?
questions.answer: IV regular insulin: Fixed-rate infusion at 0.1 U/kg/hr OR 0.14 U/kg/hr without bolus. Both approaches are equivalent. Do NOT start insulin until K+ ≥3.3 mEq/L (replace K+ first). Target glucose drop: 50-75 mg/dL/hr. When glucose <250 mg/dL, reduce insulin to 0.02-0.05 U/kg/hr and add dextrose to prevent hypoglycemia while continuing to clear ketones.

sources.text: StatPearls: Diabetic Ketoacidosis. NCBI Bookshelf. Accessed January 14, 2026.

whyChain.why: Why should insulin be withheld if K+ is <3.3 mEq/L?
whyChain.answer: Insulin drives K+ into cells. If starting K+ is already low, insulin will precipitate severe hypokalemia, which can cause fatal cardiac arrhythmias (ventricular tachycardia/fibrillation). Replace K+ aggressively until level is ≥3.3 mEq/L before starting insulin.
whyChain.source: ADA/EASD Consensus Report on Hyperglycemic Crises 2024

questions.question: What are the criteria for DKA resolution and transitioning to subcutaneous insulin?
questions.answer: DKA resolution criteria (2024 update): Glucose <200 mg/dL AND at least 2 of: (1) Venous pH >7.3, (2) HCO3 ≥18 mEq/L, (3) Anion gap ≤12 mEq/L, (4) Beta-hydroxybutyrate <1.0 mmol/L. To transition: Give SC long-acting insulin 2 hours BEFORE stopping IV insulin (overlap period prevents rebound ketosis). Patient must be eating.

sources.text: StatPearls: Diabetic Ketoacidosis. NCBI Bookshelf. Accessed January 14, 2026.

whyChain.why: Why is a 2-hour overlap between IV and SC insulin needed?
whyChain.answer: IV regular insulin has a half-life of only 5-7 minutes. If the infusion is stopped before SC insulin has absorbed and reached therapeutic levels, there will be a gap in insulin coverage, allowing lipolysis and ketogenesis to resume. The 2-hour overlap allows SC basal insulin (glargine, detemir) to reach effective levels.

questions.question: How should potassium be managed in DKA?
questions.answer: Potassium replacement is guided by serum K+ level: K+ <3.3 mEq/L: HOLD insulin, give 20-40 mEq/hr until K+ ≥3.3. K+ 3.3-5.0 mEq/L: Add 20-40 mEq K+ to each liter of IV fluid, start insulin. K+ >5.0 mEq/L: Do NOT give K+ initially, check K+ every 2 hours, start insulin. Goal: Maintain K+ between 4-5 mEq/L throughout treatment.

sources.text: StatPearls: Diabetic Ketoacidosis. NCBI Bookshelf. Accessed January 14, 2026.

whyChain.why: Why does potassium fall so rapidly during DKA treatment?
whyChain.answer: Potassium drops due to: (1) Insulin driving K+ into cells, (2) Correction of acidosis (H+ leaves cells, K+ enters), (3) Volume expansion diluting serum K+, (4) Ongoing urinary K+ losses. Total body K+ deficit is typically 300-500 mEq - aggressive replacement is needed.
whyChain.source: Management of Adult DKA. Diabetes Ther. 2014

questions.question: How often should labs be monitored during DKA treatment?
questions.answer: Initially every 1-2 hours: Glucose, electrolytes (especially K+), VBG. After stabilization (glucose falling, gap closing): Every 2-4 hours. Continue close monitoring until DKA resolution criteria are met. ECG monitoring is recommended for patients with significant hypo- or hyperkalemia.

sources.text: StatPearls: Diabetic Ketoacidosis. NCBI Bookshelf. Accessed January 14, 2026.

whyChain.why: What ECG changes indicate dangerous hypokalemia?
whyChain.answer: Progressive ECG changes with falling K+: Flattened T waves → U waves → ST depression → Prolonged QT → Ventricular arrhythmias. These changes may occur before serum K+ reaches critically low levels. If ECG changes are present, more aggressive K+ replacement is warranted.

questions.question: When is bicarbonate indicated in DKA?
questions.answer: Routine bicarbonate is NOT recommended. Consider bicarbonate only for severe acidosis with pH <7.0 (some guidelines say <6.9). Give 100 mEq NaHCO3 in 400 mL sterile water (isotonic) over 2 hours. Recheck pH and repeat if still <7.0. Bicarbonate may worsen hypokalemia and paradoxically worsen intracellular/CNS acidosis.

sources.text: StatPearls: Diabetic Ketoacidosis. NCBI Bookshelf. Accessed January 14, 2026.

whyChain.why: Why can bicarbonate cause paradoxical CNS acidosis?
whyChain.answer: When bicarbonate is given, it reacts with H+ to form CO2 and water. CO2 crosses the blood-brain barrier more readily than bicarbonate. This increases CNS CO2 and paradoxically lowers CSF pH, potentially worsening mental status. This is why bicarbonate is reserved for life-threatening acidosis.
whyChain.source: ADA/EASD Consensus Report on Hyperglycemic Crises 2024

questions.question: When should phosphate be replaced in DKA?
questions.answer: Routine phosphate replacement is NOT recommended. Consider phosphate only if: Level <1.0 mg/dL, OR patient has cardiac dysfunction, respiratory depression, or muscle weakness attributable to hypophosphatemia. Give 20-30 mEq potassium phosphate over 24 hours (provides both K+ and PO4). Monitor for hypocalcemia.

sources.text: StatPearls: Diabetic Ketoacidosis. NCBI Bookshelf. Accessed January 14, 2026.

whyChain.why: Why does phosphate fall during DKA treatment?
whyChain.answer: Like potassium, phosphate shifts extracellularly due to acidosis and insulin deficiency. Initial levels may be normal or high despite total body depletion. With insulin/glucose treatment, phosphate shifts back into cells, unmasking the deficit. Clinical hypophosphatemia develops 12-24 hours into treatment.

questions.question: What is cerebral edema in DKA, and how is it prevented and treated?
questions.answer: Cerebral edema is rare in adults (<1%) but is the leading cause of death in pediatric DKA. Risk factors: New-onset DM, severe acidosis (pH <7.1), high initial BUN, failure of sodium to rise with treatment, bicarbonate administration, rapid glucose correction. Prevention: Avoid overly aggressive fluid resuscitation in children, target gradual glucose decline (50-75 mg/dL/hr). Treatment: Mannitol 0.5-1 g/kg IV or hypertonic saline 3%.

sources.text: StatPearls: Diabetic Ketoacidosis. NCBI Bookshelf. Accessed January 14, 2026.
Kitabchi AE, et al. Hyperglycemic Crises in Adult Patients With Diabetes. Diabetes Care. 2009;32(7):1335-1343. Accessed January 14, 2026.

whyChain.why: What are early signs of cerebral edema?
whyChain.answer: Warning signs include: Headache, altered mental status, irritability, incontinence, bradycardia, hypertension (Cushing's response), and papilledema. Later signs: Pupil changes, posturing, respiratory arrest. Act immediately if suspected - do NOT wait for imaging. Give osmotic therapy empirically.

questions.question: What other complications can occur during DKA treatment?
questions.answer: Treatment-related complications: (1) Hypokalemia (most common, potentially fatal), (2) Hypoglycemia (from insulin without dextrose), (3) Hyperchloremic NAGMA (from NS), (4) Hypomagnesemia, (5) Hypophosphatemia. DKA-related complications: ARDS, rhabdomyolysis, venous thromboembolism (hypercoagulable state), aspiration (if vomiting with altered mental status), acute kidney injury.

sources.text: StatPearls: Diabetic Ketoacidosis. NCBI Bookshelf. Accessed January 14, 2026.
Kitabchi AE, et al. Hyperglycemic Crises in Adult Patients With Diabetes. Diabetes Care. 2009;32(7):1335-1343. Accessed January 14, 2026.

whyChain.why: Why are DKA patients at increased VTE risk?
whyChain.answer: DKA causes a hypercoagulable state from: Dehydration/hemoconcentration, inflammatory cytokines, endothelial dysfunction, and platelet activation. Consider VTE prophylaxis in all hospitalized DKA patients who are not actively bleeding. DVT/PE should be on the differential for DKA patients with unexplained tachycardia or hypoxia.
whyChain.source: ADA/EASD Consensus Report on Hyperglycemic Crises 2024

questions.question: How do you apply this framework to our case patient?
questions.answer: Our patient has severe DKA (pH 7.12, HCO3 8, anion gap 28). Initial management: (1) Start 0.9% NS bolus 1-1.5 L, (2) K+ is 5.8 - do NOT give potassium yet, but CAN start insulin, (3) Start insulin infusion at 0.1 U/kg/hr, (4) Monitor glucose, K+, VBG hourly, (5) Calculate corrected Na: 131 + 1.6 × (485-100)/100 = 137 (normal), (6) Add dextrose when glucose <250, (7) Add K+ to fluids when K+ <5.0.

sources.text: StatPearls: Diabetic Ketoacidosis. NCBI Bookshelf. Accessed January 14, 2026.
Kitabchi AE, et al. Hyperglycemic Crises in Adult Patients With Diabetes. Diabetes Care. 2009;32(7):1335-1343. Accessed January 14, 2026.

whyChain.why: Why can we start insulin despite K+ of 5.8?
whyChain.answer: The threshold to withhold insulin is K+ <3.3 mEq/L (low potassium). A K+ of 5.8 is elevated, so insulin is safe to start. In fact, insulin will help lower the elevated K+ by driving it into cells. We just don't add supplemental K+ to fluids until levels fall below 5.0.
whyChain.source: ADA/EASD Consensus Report on Hyperglycemic Crises 2024

questions.question: What are the key monitoring targets and endpoints in DKA management?
questions.answer: Monitoring targets: Glucose decrease 50-75 mg/dL/hr (not faster to avoid cerebral edema), K+ maintained 4-5 mEq/L, anion gap closure. Endpoints: DKA resolution (glucose <200, pH >7.3, HCO3 ≥18, gap ≤12). Then: Give SC basal insulin, wait 2 hours, stop IV insulin, ensure patient eating, address precipitant, diabetes education before discharge.

sources.text: StatPearls: Diabetic Ketoacidosis. NCBI Bookshelf. Accessed January 14, 2026.
Kitabchi AE, et al. Hyperglycemic Crises in Adult Patients With Diabetes. Diabetes Care. 2009;32(7):1335-1343. Accessed January 14, 2026.

whyChain.why: What if glucose drops too quickly (>100 mg/dL/hr)?
whyChain.answer: Rapid glucose decline increases cerebral edema risk. To slow glucose drop: (1) Add dextrose to IV fluids earlier (even if glucose >250), (2) Reduce insulin infusion rate to 0.05 U/kg/hr, (3) Increase IV fluid rate if patient is still volume depleted. Do NOT stop insulin - ketosis must be cleared.

mcqs.stem: Which of the following is the MOST important initial step in evaluating a patient with suspected diabetic ketoacidosis?

options.text: Obtain a thorough history and physical examination
options.explanation: A comprehensive history and physical examination is the foundation of clinical reasoning in diabetic ketoacidosis. It guides all subsequent diagnostic and management decisions.
Advanced imaging may be indicated but should be guided by clinical findings, not ordered reflexively.
Treatment should generally follow an appropriate diagnostic evaluation unless the patient is critically unstable.

options.text: Consult a specialist before any workup
options.explanation: Initial evaluation should be performed by the treating physician; specialist consultation follows based on findings.

mcqs.stem: A patient presents with classic features of diabetic ketoacidosis. Which clinical reasoning principle is MOST important in avoiding diagnostic error?

options.text: Search satisficing - stop looking after the first diagnosis
options.explanation: Premature closure (stopping the diagnostic process too early) is a common cognitive error that leads to missed diagnoses.
Systematic consideration of differential diagnoses, including must-not-miss conditions, is essential for avoiding premature closure and anchoring bias.
While pattern recognition (System 1 thinking) is useful, it should be balanced with analytical reasoning (System 2) to avoid cognitive biases.

options.text: Order every available test to rule out all possibilities
options.explanation: Indiscriminate testing leads to false positives, unnecessary procedures, and increased cost without improving diagnostic accuracy.

mcqs.stem: When managing diabetic ketoacidosis, which approach best supports evidence-based practice?

options.text: Follow institutional protocols regardless of patient factors
options.explanation: Evidence-based practice integrates best evidence with clinical expertise AND patient values/circumstances.

options.text: Base decisions solely on clinical experience
options.explanation: Clinical experience is valuable but can introduce bias; it should be combined with current evidence.

options.text: Integrate current best evidence with clinical expertise and patient values
options.explanation: Evidence-based medicine is the conscientious integration of best research evidence, clinical expertise, and patient values in making clinical decisions.

options.text: Always defer to the most recent published study
options.explanation: Individual studies should be appraised for quality and relevance; systematic reviews and guidelines generally provide stronger evidence.

sections.title: Clinical Approach
sections.content: Systematic approach to Diabetic Ketoacidosis including recognition, diagnosis, and management.

sections.title: Key Points
sections.content: Evidence-based clinical pearls for Diabetic Ketoacidosis.
